title: Maritime Issues and Oceans: Research & Analysis | CSIS
gist: This is a CSIS topic/section landing page for 'Maritime Issues and Oceans', not a single article. It catalogs links to reports and commentaries (US maritime industrial base / SHIPS Act, PRC pressure on Taiwan, Strait of Hormuz, Subic shipyard). Card is intentionally light; only the teaser-level signals embedded in the listing are mineable.

themes.theme: Page is a topic index, not an article

insights.claim: The CSIS 'Maritime Issues and Oceans' page is a section landing page aggregating links rather than a single analytical piece; its surfaced 2026 themes are the US maritime industrial base (MAP / SHIPS Act), PRC maritime-air coercion of Taiwan, the Strait of Hormuz, and lost shipyard capacity (Subic).
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: No standalone thesis to trade; flags CSIS's maritime-security watchlist, useful for sourcing the underlying reports individually.

insights.claim: CSIS frames US sea power as downstream of economic power, arguing the SHIPS Act and Maritime Action Plan aim to rebuild shipyards, mariners, logistics, and commercial fleets to compete with China at the industrial-base level.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Jensen and Mackenzie Eaglen
insights.so_what: Bullish framing for US/allied shipbuilding and maritime-logistics capacity as a policy priority; watch SHIPS Act funding flows.
insights.quote: Sea power begins with economic power.
insights.locator: Revisiting the Relationship Between Economic and Sea Power
